catalyst.source: This study can be viewed as a means of designing photocatalysts via the simultaneous control of surface catalytic reactivity and interfacial charge transfer in cocatalysts.
catalyst.context: Solar driven conversion of CO 2 into carbonaceous chemicals is a promising strategy to mitigate greenhouse gas emission and simultaneously store renewable energy.Rational construction of heterostructured cocatalysts represents an efficient method to improve the photocatalytic activity and selectivity in CO 2 reduction.In this work, with PdAu core-shell nanostructures with tunable Au thickness as model cocatalysts, we demonstrate the synergism of surface strain and interfacial polarization for enhanced photoreduction of CO 2 into CO.According to our experimental analysis and theory simulation, resulted from the mismatch in lattice parameters between Pd core and Au shell, compressive strain on the Au surface elevates the d-band center and improves the adsorption of key intermediate COOH.Meanwhile, charge polarization, driven by the difference in electronegativity between Pd and Au, accelerates the interfacial charge transfer and increases the electron density on Au surface.It is found that both effects are dependent on the thickness of Au shell.As a result, three-atom-thick Au shell dramatically boosts the overall efficiency in CO 2 -to-CO conversion with an impressive activity of 166.3 mol g cat -1 h -1 and selectivity of 90.6.This study can be viewed as a means of designing photocatalysts via the simultaneous control of surface catalytic reactivity and interfacial charge transfer in cocatalysts.

catalyst.source: In a typical synthesis of TiO 2 -PdAu, a solution was firstly prepared in a vial as follows
catalyst.context: Please do not adjust margins Please do not adjust margins In a typical synthesis of TiO 2 -PdAu, a solution was firstly prepared in a vial as follows: 9.625 mL aqueous solution containing 0.32 g cetyltrimethylammonium chloride (CTAC), 10 L aqueous solution of NaBr (0.01 M), 80 L aqueous solution of HAuCl 4 (10 mM) and 90 L aqueous solution of ascorbic acid (AA, 0.04 M) were mixed at 30 C to obtain a colorless solution.Subsequently, a certain amount of TiO 2 -Pd was added into the mixed solution.The solution in the vial was left undisturbed for 15 min to complete the Au growth.The resultant product was separated by centrifugation, washed with water for four times, and dried at 45 C in a vacuum.TiO 2 -PdAu samples with different Au shells were obtained through altering the amounts of TiO 2 -Pd used, including 4.5, 1.5 and 0.5 mg for the synthesis of TiO 2 -PdAu-1, TiO 2 -PdAu-2 and TiO 2 -PdAu-3, respectively. 3.0 mg as-synthesized products were dispersed in a mixture of 10 L ethanol and 10 L Nafion, which were then uniformly spin-dropped onto at a 1 cm 1 cm indium tin oxide (ITO)-coated glass by a spin coater (SC-1B, China).Subsequently, the ITO-coated glasses were heated at 80 C in a vacuum oven for 1 h.The photocurrents were measured on a CHI 760E electrochemical station (Shanghai Chenhua, China) in ambient conditions under irradiation of a 300 W Xe lamp (Solaredge 700, China).UV light (320 420 nm) was used as the illumination source, which was realized in the presence of a short-wave-pass 420 nm cutoff filter.The power density of the light was measured to be 2.7 mW cm -2 .A three-electrode cell was used to perform the electrochemical measurements.The working electrode was the ITO-coated glass.A AgAgCl electrode and Pt foil were used as the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively.The three electrodes were inserted in a quartz cell filled with 0.5 M Na 2 SO 4 electrolyte.The photoresponse of the prepared photoelectrodes (i.e., I-t) was operated by measuring the photocurrent density under chopped light irradiation (light onoff cycles: 60 s) at a bias potential of 0.4 V vs. AgAgCl for 400 s.Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in the 10 -1 to 10 5 Hz frequency range with an AC voltage amplitude of 10 mV at an applied potential of 0.4 V vs. AgAgCl.The transient open-circuit voltage decay (OCVD) was measured for a total of 800 s under UV light irradiation, and the light was switched on and off 100 and 400 s from the start, respectively.The average lifetime of the photogenerated carriers ( n ) was obtained according to the OCVD using the following equation: where K B is the Boltzmann constant, T is the temperature (in Kelvin), and q is the unsigned charge of an electron. To investigate the photocatalytic activities of TiO 2 -based photocatalysts for CO 2 reduction, 15 mg of photocatalysts were dispersed on the flat glass plate at the bottom of photocatalytic reactor.And 5 mL H 2 O was added and surrounded the plate.Prior to the test, the reactor loaded with catalyst was first purged with high purity CO 2 (0.15 MPa) for 20 min.Then the light-irradiation experiment was performed by using a 300 W Xe lamp with UV light as the illumination source.The power density of UV light (320 420 nm) was measured to be 2.7 mW cm -2 .The photocatalytic reaction was performed under irradiation of UV light with stirring at 300 rpm for 4 h.After completion of the reaction, the gaseous mixture was analyzed using a gas chromatograph (GC-2014, Ar carrier, Shimadzu) with Ar as the carrier gas.The amount of H 2 and O 2 was determined using a thermal conductivity detector (TCD).The amount of CH 4 was measured by using a flame ionization detector (FID).The amount of CO was converted to CH 4 by using a methanation reactor and then analyzed by using the FID.After the reaction was completed, the sample was collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min.During the stability test, the photocatalyst with the best performance was collected after each run and then reused for photoreduction of CO 2 .The photocatalyst was heat treated at 160 C for 3 h after the runs before its reusing.The isotope-labeling experiments were performed using 13 CO 2 instead of 12 CO 2 , and the products were analyzed using gas chromatographymass spectrometry (7890B5977A, Agilent).Visible photocatalytic performance was measured under the same experimental condition except that both long-wave-pass 420-nm and short-wave-pass 780nm cutoff filters (420 780 nm ) were used instead of shortwave-pass 420 nm ( 420 nm) cutoff filter.

catalyst.source: Fig. 4 (a) Comparison of the H 2 , CO and CH 4 production rate as well as selectivity for CO production over TiO 2 based photocatalysts under UV light irradiation
catalyst.context: In summary, we have demonstrated the synergistic interplay between surface strain and interfacial polarization in photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with PdAu core-shell cocatalyst as a well-defined platform.The photocatalytic performance of PdAu cocatalysts integrated with TiO 2 turns out to have a strong correlation with the Au shell thickness.Remarkably, by substantially reducing the Au thickness into three atomic layers, TiO 2 -PdAu-1 achieves a high selectivity of 90.6 for CO production with an average rate of 166.3 mol g cat -1 h -1 .The combination of experimental analyses and theory simulations reveals that the compressive strain on the Au surface shifts up the d-band center and strengthens the adsorption of key intermediate COOH, while charge polarization promotes interfacial electron transfer from Pd to Au.This work not only offers a method of collaborative utilization of surface strain and interfacial polarization in photocatalysis, but also enables the design of more active and highly selective photocatalysts toward solardriven CO 2 conversion at atomic precision based on the united engineering on surface and interface of cocatalyst. Fig.2(a) High-magnification and (b) Atomic-resolution HAADF-STEM images of a PdAu core-shell nanocrystal in TiO 2 -PdAu-1; (c-e) HAADF-STEM images and corresponding EDS mapping profiles of (c) TiO 2 -PdAu-1, (d) TiO 2 -PdAu-2 and (e) TiO 2 -PdAu-3. Fig. 3 (a) XRD patterns of TiO 2 based samples; (b,c) XPS spectra of TiO 2 based samples: (b) Pd3d Au4d, (c) Au4f high-resolution spectra. Fig. 4 (a) Comparison of the H 2 , CO and CH 4 production rate as well as selectivity for CO production over TiO 2 based photocatalysts under UV light irradiation; (b) activity and selectivity for CO production over TiO 2 -PdAu-1 photocatalyst in five successive cycles; (c) mass spectrum of 13 CO produced over TiO 2 -PdAu-1 under UV light irradiation. Fig. 5 (a) UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra of TiO 2 based samples; (b) I-t curves of TiO 2 based samples under UV light irradiation; (c) EIS Nyquist plots; (d) PL spectra at 315 nm; (e) transient OCVD measurements after exposure to UV light; (f) average lifetimes of the photogenerated charge carriers ( n ) obtained from transient OCVD measurements. Fig. 7 (a) Free energy change of each elementary step on Au(100) under different surface strains; (b) PDOS of surface atoms on Au(100) surfaces under different surface strains.The calculated d-band centers are marked with black lines. Photocatalytic H 2 , CO, CH 4 and O 2 production rate as well as selectivity for CO production over TiO 2 based photocatalysts under UV light irradiation. This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21603191, 21872125) and Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LY20B030003, LQ16B010001). There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

co-catalyst.source: In this work, with PdAu core-shell nanostructures with tunable Au thickness as model cocatalysts, we demonstrate the synergism of surface strain and interfacial polarization for enhanced photoreduction of CO 2 into CO.
co-catalyst.context: Solar driven conversion of CO 2 into carbonaceous chemicals is a promising strategy to mitigate greenhouse gas emission and simultaneously store renewable energy.Rational construction of heterostructured cocatalysts represents an efficient method to improve the photocatalytic activity and selectivity in CO 2 reduction.In this work, with PdAu core-shell nanostructures with tunable Au thickness as model cocatalysts, we demonstrate the synergism of surface strain and interfacial polarization for enhanced photoreduction of CO 2 into CO.According to our experimental analysis and theory simulation, resulted from the mismatch in lattice parameters between Pd core and Au shell, compressive strain on the Au surface elevates the d-band center and improves the adsorption of key intermediate COOH.Meanwhile, charge polarization, driven by the difference in electronegativity between Pd and Au, accelerates the interfacial charge transfer and increases the electron density on Au surface.It is found that both effects are dependent on the thickness of Au shell.As a result, three-atom-thick Au shell dramatically boosts the overall efficiency in CO 2 -to-CO conversion with an impressive activity of 166.3 mol g cat -1 h -1 and selectivity of 90.6.This study can be viewed as a means of designing photocatalysts via the simultaneous control of surface catalytic reactivity and interfacial charge transfer in cocatalysts.

of a 300 W Xe lamp (Solaredge 700, China).UV light (320 420 nm) was used as the illumination source, which was realized in the presence of a short-wave-pass 420 nm cutoff filter.The power density of the light was measured to be 2.7 mW cm -2 .A three-electrode cell was used to perform the electrochemical measurements.The working electrode was the ITO-coated glass.A AgAgCl electrode and Pt foil s.Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in the 10 -1 to 10 5 Hz frequency range with an AC voltage amplitude of 10 mV at an applied potential of 0.4 V vs. AgAgCl.The transient open-circuit voltage decay (OCVD) was measured for a total of 800 s under UV light irradiation, and the light was switched on and off 100 and 400 s from the start, respectively.The average lifetime of the photogenerated carriers ( n ) was obtained according to the OCVD using the following equation: where K B is the Boltzmann constant, T is the temperature (in Kelvin), and q is the unsigned charge of an electron. To investigate the photocatalytic activities of TiO 2 -based photocatalysts for CO 2 reduction, 15 mg of photocatalysts were dispersed on the flat glass plate at the bottom of photocatalytic reactor.And 5 mL H 2 O was added and surrounded the plate.Prior to the test, the reactor loaded with catalyst was first purged with high purity CO 2 (0.15 MPa) for 20 min.Then the light-irradiation experiment was performed by using a 300 W Xe lamp with UV light as the illumination source.The power density of UV light (320 420 nm) was measured to be 2.7 mW cm -2 .The photocatalytic reaction was performed under irradiation of UV light with stirring at 300 rpm for 4 h.After completion of the reaction, the gaseous mixture was analyzed using a gas chromatograph (GC-2014, Ar carrier, Shimadzu) with Ar as the carrier gas.The amount of H 2 and O 2 was determined using a thermal conductivity detector (TCD).The amount of CH 4 was measured by using a flame ionization detector (FID).The amount of CO was converted to CH 4 by using a methanation reactor and then analyzed by using the FID.After the reaction was completed, the sample was collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min.During the stability test, the photocatalyst with the best performance was collected after each run and then reused for photoreduction of CO 2 .The photocatalyst was heat treated at 160 C for 3 h after the runs before its reusing.The isotope-labeling experiments were performed using 13 CO 2 instead of 12 CO 2 , and the products were analyzed using gas chromatographymass spectrometry (7890B5977A, Agilent).Visible photocatalytic performance was measured under the same experimental condition except that both long-wave-pass 420-nm and short-wave-pass 780nm cutoff filters (420 780 nm ) were used instead of shortwave-pass 420 nm ( 420 nm) cutoff filter.

co-catalyst.source: In summary, we have demonstrated the synergistic interplay between surface strain and interfacial polarization in photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with PdAu core-shell cocatalyst as a well-defined platform.
co-catalyst.context: In summary, we have demonstrated the synergistic interplay between surface strain and interfacial polarization in photocatalytic CO 2 reduction with PdAu core-shell cocatalyst as a well-defined platform.The photocatalytic performance of PdAu cocatalysts integrated with TiO 2 turns out to have a strong correlation with the Au shell thickness.Remarkably, by substantially reducing the Au thickness into three atomic layers, TiO 2 -PdAu-1 achieves a high selectivity of 90.6 for CO production with an average rate of 166.3 mol g cat -1 h -1 .The combination of experimental analyses and theory simulations reveals that the compressive strain on the Au surface shifts up the d-band center and strengthens the adsorption of key intermediate COOH, while charge polarization promotes interfacial electron transfer from Pd to Au.This work not only offers a method of collaborative utilization of surface strain and interfacial polarization in photocatalysis, but also enables the design of more active and highly selective photocatalysts toward solardriven CO 2 conversion at atomic precision based on the united engineering on surface and interface of cocatalyst. Fig.2(a) High-magnification and (b) Atomic-resolution HAADF-STEM images of a PdAu core-shell nanocrystal in TiO 2 -PdAu-1; (c-e) HAADF-STEM images and corresponding EDS mapping profiles of (c) TiO 2 -PdAu-1, (d) TiO 2 -PdAu-2 and (e) TiO 2 -PdAu-3. Fig. 3 (a) XRD patterns of TiO 2 based samples; (b,c) XPS spectra of TiO 2 based samples: (b) Pd3d Au4d, (c) Au4f high-resolution spectra. Fig. 4 (a) Comparison of the H 2 , CO and CH 4 production rate as well as selectivity for CO production over TiO 2 based photocatalysts under UV light irradiation; (b) activity and selectivity for CO production over TiO 2 -PdAu-1 photocatalyst in five successive cycles; (c) mass spectrum of 13 CO produced over TiO 2 -PdAu-1 under UV light irradiation. Fig. 5 (a) UV-vis diffuse reflectance spectra of TiO 2 based samples; (b) I-t curves of TiO 2 based samples under UV light irradiation; (c) EIS Nyquist plots; (d) PL spectra at 315 nm; (e) transient OCVD measurements after exposure to UV light; (f) average lifetimes of the photogenerated charge carriers ( n ) obtained from transient OCVD measurements. Fig. 7 (a) Free energy change of each elementary step on Au(100) under different surface strains; (b) PDOS of surface atoms on Au(100) surfaces under different surface strains.The calculated d-band centers are marked with black lines. Photocatalytic H 2 , CO, CH 4 and O 2 production rate as well as selectivity for CO production over TiO 2 based photocatalysts under UV light irradiation. This work is supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21603191, 21872125) and Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China (No. LY20B030003, LQ16B010001). There are no conflicts of interest to declare.

ITO-coated glasses were heated at 80 C in a vacuum oven for 1 h.The photocurrents were measured on a CHI 760E electrochemical station (Shanghai Chenhua, China) in ambient conditions under irradiation of a 300 W Xe lamp (Solaredge 700, China).UV light (320 420 nm) was used as the illumination source, which was realized in the presence of a short-wave-pass 420 nm cutoff filter.The power density of the light was measured to be 2.7 mW cm -2 .A three-electrode cell was used to perform the electrochemical measurements.The working electrode was the ITO-coated glass.A AgAgCl electrode and Pt foil were used as the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively.The three electrodes were inserted in a quartz cell filled with 0.5 M Na 2 SO 4 electrolyte.The photoresponse of the prepared photoelectrodes (i.e., I-t) was operated by measuring the photocurrent density under chopped light irradiation (light onoff cycles: 60 s) at a bias potential of 0.4 V vs. AgAgCl for 400 s.Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed in the 10 -1 to 10 5 Hz frequency range with an AC voltage amplitude of 10 mV at an applied potential of 0.4 V vs. AgAgCl.The transient open-circuit voltage decay (OCVD) was measured for a total of 800 s under UV light irradiation, and the light was switched on and off 100 and 400 s from the start, respectively.The average lifetime of the photogenerated carriers ( n ) was obtained according to the OCVD using the following equation: where K B is the Boltzmann constant, T is the temperature (in Kelvin), and q is the unsigned charge of an electron. To investigate the photocatalytic activities of TiO 2 -based photocatalysts for CO 2 reduction, 15 mg of photocatalysts were dispersed on the flat glass plate at the bottom of photocatalytic reactor.And 5 mL H 2 O was added and surrounded the plate.Prior to the test, the reactor loaded with catalyst was first purged with high purity CO 2 (0.15 MPa) for 20 min.Then the light-irradiation experiment was performed by using a 300 W Xe lamp with UV light as the illumination source.The power density of UV light (320 420 nm) was measured to be 2.7 mW cm -2 .The photocatalytic reaction was performed under irradiation of UV light with stirring at 300 rpm for 4 h.After completion of the reaction, the gaseous mixture was analyzed using a gas chromatograph (GC-2014, Ar carrier, Shimadzu) with Ar as the carrier gas.The amount of H 2 and O 2 was determined using a thermal conductivity detector (TCD).The amount of CH 4 was measured by using a flame ionization detector (FID).The amount of CO was converted to CH 4 by using a methanation reactor and then analyzed by using the FID.After the reaction was completed, the sample was collected by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 5 min.During the stability test, the photocatalyst with the best performance was collected after each run and then reused for photoreduction of CO 2 .The photocatalyst was heat treated at 160 C for 3 h after the runs before its reusing.The isotope-labeling experiments were performed using 13 CO 2 instead of 12 CO 2 , and the products were analyzed using gas chromatographymass spectrometry (7890B5977A, Agilent).Visible photocatalytic performance was measured under the same experimental condition except that both long-wave-pass 420-nm and short-wave-pass 780nm cutoff filters (420 780 nm ) were used instead of shortwave-pass 420 nm ( 420 nm) cutoff filter.
